dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3, our location is a 12 year old male unresponsive at --Address Redacted--.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
He just collapsed and started shaking a few minutes ago.
Is he conscious, can he open his eyes for me?
No, he is not responding at all. Like, he's completely still.
We're going to need to do a quick check on his airway. Can you step back a bit?
Airway is patent, no signs of obstruction, and tongue is clear. No signs of choking.
Just checked his breathing. Chest is rising and falling, equal on both sides.
Respirations at 14, regular and steady.
Pulse is 90 right now.
Blood pressure is 108 over 59.
Pulse ox is 98%.
EKG tracing shows a sinus rhythm.
Blood glucose is 105 mg/dL at the moment.
Pupils are round, equal, and reactive. Would you be okay if we test your eyes for a second?
Yes, go on then.
Light shone in each eye, both pupils are reactive, normal. No signs of injury or trauma.
I will ask him a few questions. His condition is unresponsive.
I'll tap his shoulder and see if he moves. No response.
Glasgow Coma Scale: 5 (eye opening 1, verbal response 1, motor response 3). He's noted to have NARD.
We're going to ask his guardian about his medical history. Can you help with that?
Sure. He has asthma and is on medication, but I can't remember the name.
What about any allergies?
He is allergic to just about everything - eggs, nuts, shellfish, wheat, and lots of pollen stuff.
Can you tell me what medications he is currently taking?
He is on Benadryl and Hydroxyzine for his allergies and behavior.
What about food or time? What's the last he ate or drank?
He had a sandwich for lunch and left it uneaten recently. No drinks in the last hour.
What happened just before he started shaking? Can you describe the event?
I think he was playing with something in the kitchen and maybe had contact with something he's allergic to.
Got it. His age is 12, male. Any other health issues we should note?
He's autistic. It can affect his communication a lot.
We'll need to check for pain, but since he's unresponsive, I'm assuming he's not in pain.
I'm going to check his skin. Can you keep him still?
Yes, he is not moving at all.
Skin is pale, cool, and diaphoretic. Would you be okay if I do a more detailed check on his skin?
I guess so.
No rashes, cuts, or signs of trauma. No signs of a bite or sting on visible areas.
No signs of respiratory distress.
Pupils still PERRL, no signs of rigidity or eye issues.
Field impression suggests a seizure episode, not anaphylaxis. All signs are consistent with a post-seizure state. He is noted to be A&O x4/4 upon later assessment.
We'll proceed with protocol for altered mental status. Transport immediately to the hospital is key.
I am now going to attempt a 12-lead EKG, but it's difficult due to him being diaphoretic.
12-lead EKG was attempted but could not be obtained due to patient being diaphoretic.
Rechecking capnography, but not cooperating due to his current state.
We'll take him to VCU Health Systems for pediatric care.
Second glucose reading shows zero. No read due to patient not cooperating.
Pulse is now 98, seems to be maintaining.
Rate of 16 now, still consistent.
Blood pressure climbed to 120 over 63.
Oxygen saturation is 100%, improved.
Rechecking a 4-lead EKG now, as patient became more alert during this phase.
4-lead EKG shows a sinus rhythm, no ST abnormalities.
Let me repeat his vitals quickly before transportation - to updated monitoring.
He is still not in any pain. Patient is now more alert but was unresponsive earlier per GCS measures.
Just to double check, can I get a recap of what he was doing before the episode?
He was in the kitchen, messing with the cutting board and knives. I think something may have aggravated him.
Thanks. No signs of injury from that activity found. No mention of choking during any part of the call.
Again, I'm securing him. Tranquil and safe for transport.
We need you in the emergency room at VCU. He is a 12-year-old male with altered mental status and seizure-like episodes.
His vitals are stable now. We have a drug history, allergens, and a small improvement in level of awareness.
Altered mental status and seizure are the main issues. We did find he was alert and orientated to person, place, time, and event.
He is A&O x4 by the time of arrival. That's critical to note in transfer records.
Did he have any cold or flu-like symptoms recently?
No, he's been fine all week. Just this sudden episode.
How about any chest pain or shortness of breath?
He didn't mention any of that. This all began with the shaking and unconsciousness.
No signs of respiratory distress.
Was he alert and oriented just prior to the episodes?
Yes, he was playing earlier. It was sudden. No signs he had an issue before.
Rechecked breathing again: 14 breaths per minute, equal on both sides.
Pupils still PERRL, no signs of rigidity or eye issues.
What's the usual behavior and communication style for him, since he is autistic?
He's on the higher end of the spectrum. Can understand and speak - just more reserved.
Thanks, that's helpful. No signs of choking or difficulty breathing observed.
EKG shows stable sinus rhythm. No cardiac anomalies found.
12-lead EKG was attempted but we couldn't get a full reading.
Repeat breathing: 16, no changes from the last reading.
Still no usable glucose reading. Attempting another sample now.
Skin remains normal in color now, warm, and dry. No abnormal turgor.
I found no obstruction or foreign body in the throat.
No signs of nasal discharge, bleeding, or noticeable obstruction in the mouth.
Breathing is equal bilaterally. No signs of trauma or chest pain.
Abdomen not visibly inspected, but no signs of distension or acute pain.
Patient does not appear to show signs of extremity trauma or spinal discomfort.
Rechecking 4-lead EKG now, as patient became more alert during transport.
All systems are baselined and looking stable. We will enter VCU ER.
He should be in good hands at the hospital. Any final thoughts for the urgent care?
I'd strongly note the allergy history and current medications, as well as his baseline neurologic status.
Also, record the time he became alert and orientated x4; that was a major indication.
Medical notes on attempted 12-lead and capnography are very important in the charting.
Everything has been documented, photograph taken, and signed off by guardian. All care is now with the ED.
Ready for handover to hospital staff. I'll stay with him to ensure full transfer.
Want me to follow with a copy of the patient's medical history and allergies?
Yes, that's very important given his multiple allergens.
Use some padding during loading to keep him as comfortable as possible.
Moving him onto the stretcher with the help of two crew members. All tied down and secured.
Transport via ambulance in monitored condition. Vital signs must be tracked en route.
EMS is en route, patient remains in sinus rhythm and has sustained breathing and vitals.
No deterioration observed in patient's condition thus far. All systems are functional.
I'll begin the handover now with hospital staff. I'll walk down with him to make sure all details are provided.
You ready ambulance side?
Ready. Taking stretcher to ER. Care transferred officially.
